key_insight: Realistic piezoelectric-induced wall deformations can manipulate near-wall turbulence, with spanwise waves delivering the strongest drag reduction.
engineering_relevance: This work suggests a practical surface-actuation route for energy-efficient flow control in channels, ducts, and other turbulent-wall applications.
method: Performed direct numerical simulations of turbulent half-channel flow at Reτ = 200.
Modeled piezoelectric actuation with finite element analysis over 119–543 Hz and 250–500 V.
Applied resulting wall deformations as moving bottom-wall boundary conditions to generate upstream, downstream, and spanwise waveforms.
evidence_signal: The abstract reports drag reduction measurements across actuation cases, with maxima of 1.6% for upstream, 5.4% for downstream, and 27.6% for spanwise waves.
watch_item: The reported gains are for a low-Reynolds-number DNS setup, so real-world performance may differ due to scaling, fabrication tolerances, and actuator constraints.
paper_title: Direct numerical simulations of turbulent drag reduction via piezoelectric actuation
